summary: Task-11 delivers the EffectiveConfig merge, D15 classification, three apply* state machines, D12 heavy-change/reinit dialogs and the TopAppBar integration; module boundaries are clean (core-runtime/core-settings have zero Compose/Activity imports), the stopâ†’cleanupâ†’initialize sequence is verified by the test `applyHeavySetting_sequencing_stopCleanupInitialize`, and `_reinitInProgress` is correctly managed inside a try/finally. No critical or major blockers. Findings are minor: one dead LaunchedEffect, a redundant defensive stopResponse, state that won't survive rotation, an unused import, and a cosmetic concurrency note about `model.configValues` lacking @Volatile (not a real race today because both writers and readers sit on viewModelScope's Main dispatcher while `_reinitInProgress` gates `send`).

findings.description: `LaunchedEffect(reinitInProgress)` body is a comment only â€” it documents an intent ('auto-dismiss the sheet once the reinit completes') but the effect does nothing. In practice the sheet's `onDismiss()` is already invoked inline after `saveAndApplySettings` / `resetSettingsToDefaults` / `HeavyChangeDialog.onConfirm`, so the sheet closes before the reinit starts and the effect is redundant. The `reinitInProgress` state and its `collectAsStateWithLifecycle` subscription also become dead weight.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove both the `val reinitInProgress by viewModel.reinitInProgress.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()` line and the `LaunchedEffect(reinitInProgress)` block. If the auto-dismiss is actually required, replace the empty body with `if (!reinitInProgress) onDismiss()` gated on a prior `sheetOpenAt: Long` â€” but given the current onDismiss sites, the effect is genuinely unnecessary.

findings.description: `applyHeavySetting` calls `helper.stopResponse(priorModel)` before `registry.cleanup(modelName)`. But `DefaultModelRegistry.releaseEngine` (invoked by `cleanup`) already calls `llmModelHelper.stopResponse(model)` itself (DefaultModelRegistry.kt:262) before closing. So the explicit stopResponse here is redundant. Not harmful â€” both paths are idempotent (`conversation.cancelProcess()`) â€” but it's duplicated work and a small maintenance hazard: a future change to the registry's teardown order could silently invert the sequence expected here. Note: the test `applyHeavySetting_sequencing_stopCleanupInitialize` requires the explicit call because its `FakeModelRegistry.cleanup` does *not* forward to `stopResponse` â€” so removing the VM-side stopResponse would drop the first entry in `sharedCalls` and the assertion would need to be updated.
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (preserve intent, make the redundancy explicit): add a KDoc line on the stopResponse call noting that this is defence-in-depth for fakes and a future-proof guard if `cleanup` ever stops forwarding to stopResponse. Option B (drop the redundancy): delete lines 358-361 and update `applyHeavySetting_sequencing_stopCleanupInitialize` to assert `listOf("cleanup", "initialize")` â€” the registry's real cleanup already stops, which the test can't exercise directly because the fake registry mocks `cleanup` as a pure counter.

findings.description: `var pendingHeavyApply by remember { mutableStateOf<HeavyAction?>(null) }` uses plain `remember`, not `rememberSaveable`. If the user rotates the device while `HeavyChangeDialog` is shown, the dialog state is lost and the dialog disappears without confirming or cancelling â€” the user's Apply click is silently dropped. Same concern applies to the field state (`systemPrompt`, `temperature` etc., though those are `remember(effective)` and will repopulate from the effective merge on rotation). `HeavyAction.Save(target: PerModelSettings)` holds a proto which is parcelable via `MessageLite` but not `Parcelable` â€” so migrating to `rememberSaveable` needs a custom `Saver`.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a custom `Saver<HeavyAction?, *>` that serialises `HeavyAction.Save.target` via `target.toByteArray()` / `PerModelSettings.parseFrom(bytes)`, then switch to `rememberSaveable(saver = ...)`; or (b) accept the loss and document it in KDoc â€” rotation mid-dialog is rare and the user can simply re-open the sheet. Given Phase-2's scope and the precedent set in task-8 (camera sheet rotation) I'd accept (b) with a KDoc note.

findings.description: `classifyApplyLevel` does `current[key] != target[key]` across the 7 fields. There is a type drift between allowlist defaults and overrides: `createLlmChatConfigs` (Config.kt:203, 221) produces `MAX_TOKENS` as `String` ("1024") when `defaultMaxContextLength` is null and `TOPK` as `Float` (`defaultTopK.toFloat()`), while `EffectiveConfig.merge` writes the proto's Int value back. After the very first Apply (even a no-op Apply where the user didn't change anything), `current[TOPK]` is Int (from the previously-saved override) while `target[TOPK]` is also Int â€” fine. But on a fresh install with no override, `current[TOPK] = 40.0f (Float)` and `target[TOPK] = 40 (Int)` â€” `40.0f != 40` is true in Kotlin (different runtime classes), so every first-Apply is classified as LIGHT, never NONE. Not a crash, not even a wrong UX (the light apply is a no-op outside of the config map reassignment), but worth tightening to avoid downstream confusion if a future feature branches on `ApplyLevel.NONE`.
findings.suggested_fix: Normalise both sides before comparison. Simplest: introduce a private `typedEquals(a: Any?, b: Any?): Boolean` that coerces numeric types (Int/Float/Double/String) before `==`. Or, better, fix the root cause in `createLlmChatConfigs` so `MAX_TOKENS`/`TOPK` defaults are always `Int`/`Float` matching the proto field types. Scope guard: fixing `createLlmChatConfigs` is a Phase-2 cross-cut and will ripple into `AllowlistLoaderTest`; the VM-local typedEquals is the narrower fix.

findings.description: `model.configValues = EffectiveConfig.merge(...)` is called from three coroutines (`applyLightOverrides`, `applyHeavySetting`, `applySystemPromptAndReset`, and the `init { applyEffectiveConfigToModel() }` block). `configValues` is a plain `var` on `data class Model` â€” no `@Volatile`, no atomic. All VM call sites launch on `viewModelScope` which defaults to `Dispatchers.Main.immediate`, and `ChatViewModel.send()` also reads `model.configValues[ENABLE_THINKING.label]` from Main â€” so under the Main-single-threaded invariant the reassignment is safe and the JMM provides the necessary happens-before via Main's queue ordering. But `DefaultModelRegistry.initialize` hops to `Dispatchers.Default` (line 171) and writes `model.configValues + (ACCELERATOR to CPU.label)` on line 197 during CPU-fallback. That write is serialised behind `lifecycleMutex` but crosses Defaultâ†’Main boundary to become visible to subsequent send() reads. Under the current design the mutex release + coroutine resume provides the happens-before, so there's no real race today â€” but the field invites a future regression. Flagging for documentation, not for code change.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a class-level KDoc on `Model.configValues` noting the Main-thread invariant and the registry's CPU-fallback write-after-mutex path, so a future contributor doesn't introduce a background writer that violates the invariant. No code change strictly required.

findings.description: Import order â€” `import androidx.compose.foundation.text.KeyboardOptions` is placed after the `androidx.lifecycle.compose` / `app.sanctum.machina.*` imports instead of grouped with the other `androidx.compose.foundation` imports at the top. Small ktlint-style nit.
findings.suggested_fix: Move line 45 up into the `androidx.compose.foundation` block (around line 10). Non-blocking.

findings.description: `AcceleratorField` hardcodes `options = listOf("GPU", "CPU")` instead of deriving from `model.accelerators` / `Accelerator` enum. If a model's allowlist entry ever advertises NPU or a different ordering, the sheet will silently fail to expose it â€” and the `buildOverrides()` path will write whatever string the user chose back into the proto. Phase-2 only targets GPU/CPU on Honor 200, but hardcoding the labels duplicates truth with `Accelerator.GPU.label` / `Accelerator.CPU.label`.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) pull the two labels from `Accelerator.GPU.label` and `Accelerator.CPU.label` constants; or (b) plumb `supportedAccelerators: List<Accelerator>` through `ChatViewModel.modelCaps` so the sheet reflects the model's allowlist. (a) is a 2-line change and a sufficient fix for Phase-2 scope; (b) is Phase-3.

findings.description: Double blank line between `dispatchByLevel` and `applyEffectiveConfigToModel` (lines 544-546). Cosmetic.

`fun observePerModelSettings(): kotlinx.coroutines.flow.Flow<PerModelSettings?>` uses a fully qualified `Flow` return type instead of the already-imported `kotlinx.coroutines.flow` namespace (line 31 imports `first`, `update`, etc. from that package).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.Flow` at the top and simplify the return type to `Flow<PerModelSettings?>`.

dimensions_checked.architecture: OK â€” module boundaries clean, grep on core-runtime and core-settings for androidx.compose/androidx.activity returns zero hits. EffectiveConfig is a pure function with LinkedHashMap return; stays in :app next to the bottom sheet and VM that consume it (consistent with tech-spec Decision D16).
dimensions_checked.separation_of_concerns: OK â€” ChatViewModel holds state-machine logic; the sheet is pure UI + state hoist; dialogs are stateless composables; EffectiveConfig is a pure merge. Class sizes: ChatViewModel 614 lines (within project norm â€” Phase-2 VM carries all UI surfaces), InferenceSettingsBottomSheet 342 lines with small private composables.
dimensions_checked.readability: OK â€” KDoc on every public method references the decision label (D12/D15/D16/D21/D23/D24). Internal helpers (`dispatchByLevel`, `classifyApplyLevel`, `computeDefaults`, `effectiveSystemPrompt`, `preInitModel`) are clearly named.
dimensions_checked.error_handling: OK â€” `applyHeavySetting` try/finally on `_reinitInProgress`, `errorLog.e("inference-init", ...)` on failure, snackbar surfaces `chat_load_failed_title`. Init-crash test `applyHeavySetting_initCrash_failedState` green.
dimensions_checked.type_safety: Minor drift â€” `classifyApplyLevel` compares heterogeneous numeric types from the legacy `createLlmChatConfigs` Int-as-String / Int-as-Float defaults against the proto Int/Float overrides (finding #4).
dimensions_checked.testing_coverage: Adequate â€” EffectiveConfig 8 anchors, ChatViewModel 25 tests including all 12 Task-11 TDD anchors. stopâ†’cleanupâ†’initialize sequencing and init-crash transition both covered. No Compose UI tests (excluded by Phase-2 testing strategy).
dimensions_checked.dependencies: Correct â€” `protobuf-javalite` promotion from `implementation` to `api` in `core-settings/build.gradle.kts:53` is required for `:app` to call `PerModelSettings.hasXxx()` / `newBuilder().setXxx()` through the `AppSettingsRepository` surface. KDoc comment on line 52 explains the rationale.
dimensions_checked.performance: OK â€” `applyLightOverrides` reassigns a small map, no reinit; heavy path is gated behind the modal progress dialog; no hot-path allocations introduced.
dimensions_checked.cross_file_consistency: Verified â€” `ModelRegistry.cleanup(modelName)`, `initialize(modelName): Result<Unit>`, `resetConversation(modelName, systemPrompt)` signatures match ChatViewModel call sites. `AppSettingsRepository.{observe,save,reset}PerModelSettings(modelId)` signatures match. `LlmModelHelper.stopResponse(model)` matches. `PerModelSettings.hasXxx()`/`setXxx()` methods generated by protobuf-lite (verified via core-settings proto schema in task-2 decisions).
dimensions_checked.resource_management: OK â€” no new heavy resources. `_reinitInProgress` is a simple StateFlow; the `try { ... } finally { _reinitInProgress.value = false }` in `applyHeavySetting` guarantees the modal dismisses even on exception.
